speaker: interviewer
text: And OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Friday, February 3, 1995. And this is an interview with survivor Esther Bem. The interviewer is Sandi Pelly, and it's being conducted in Toronto, Canada, in English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Camera's set.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you start by telling me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Esther Bem. I was born Esther Svabenic. And I was born in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in former Yugoslavia, in Zagreb, which is Croatia now. It was Croatia even then. And I knew at a very early age that I wasn't born into quiet times. It was felt in the home because I-- my mother was originally from Austria. And there was a big family in Austria who started to write for help and, and what to do with themselves. And some of them came in 1937 and 1938. And they lived with my grandmother. And my parents didn't take them in, but they lived-- they were entertained by us, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'd like to go back a little bit when you were a young child. Can you tell me what it was like in Zagreb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zagreb was a very pleasant city to live. It was a big city-- culture. And Jews were quite assimilated. I come from a family that was semi-Orthodox and atheistic. My father was not religious. And my mother was a daughter of a rabbi. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were people that loved each other. And this was felt in our life, with-- in our family life. It was a very good life. I had two older sisters, 11 and 9 years older. And my whole life was learning from them and watching them and being-- imitating them. The older sister was very intellectual, very scholarly. And she took a big interest in me. And I was kind of growing up under her wings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was the Jelka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your other sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other sister was Vera. She was athletic. She spent half of her life in the gym. She was a, a long-distance runner. She was an outdoor person. And actually, being an outdoor person helped her to survive the war because she ended up in the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father. What, what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Arthur. He was an engineer. He was semi-intellectual. He always read and always tried to advance intellectually. He had a business, a real-estate business. And since he was an engineer, he was doing some building additions to to factories. And I have to say we had a very good upper-middle-class life. We were sheltered. We lived in a wonderful part of the city-- which turned out later not to be so good. It would have been better if we had been in a poorer part of the city-- very close to the theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I spent my time with my sisters going to the shows. They always took me. I was introduced very early in life to opera and ballet, thanks to my parents who liked that kind of stuff. And I was going with them. They never let-- left me home. What was your house like that you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in an apartment in a very nice part of the city on the fourth floor with elevator. We had a lot of books. We had a piano. We had a maid. It was a nice, European life in a nice city. There was a big family. My mother had sisters and mother. And my father had brothers. And it was quite a active family life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you spend a lot of time with your aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a cousin who was my age. His mother wasn't Jewish-- my father's brother. And my-- I-- all my childhood I spent with them because every afternoon his father came with him to visit us. So we were playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin's name was Robert. And my uncle's name was Otto. I went to Jewish school in Zagreb. So I would say that most of my friends were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most about going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a good school. We, we were taught Bible. We were taught Jewish holidays. We had place for Purim or for Hanukkah. We had a wonderful teacher, which was unforgettable. And she was, like-- the way she was kept me alive mentally after, when I didn't go to school for many years. I was thinking often how she gave me in those four years more, more than called for and more than expected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Mrs. Schreistein. And I know that she died only a few year ago in an old-folks' home in Zagreb.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Jewish holidays? Who did you spend them with? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish holidays were kind of a strange thing in our family. My father belonged to a totally Reformed synagogue. And my mother, [LAUGHS] because of her brother and sisters, she belonged to an Orthodox synagogue. So one day we went to the Orthodox, one day we went to the Reformed. And it didn't matter to me. I was religious for-- until I was nine. And then listening to my sisters, who were very progressive and socialist, I decided I think this is better to be on their side. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in 1941, [SIGHS] everything changed. I'd, I'd like to go back a little bit before we get into the war. Did you experience anti-Semitism growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: None.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand that being Jewish was going to be a problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were-- we lived, as I said before, sheltered life, protected. And going to-- not Hebrew, but we learned Hebrew in school-- to the Jewish school, there were only Jewish children in that school. I-- practically, except my cousin who was half-Jewish and lived like a non-Jew, I didn't know anything about anti-Semitism except for they were talking in the house, in my home, about what's going on now in Austria and Poland. And my parents were listening to the radio. And they were very scared. And there were signs in the newspaper, in the propaganda, of the country that we are going into a new direction, which was still totally unknown to us. And-- although, I must say that my father foresaw that things are not good. And he did not go into denial. And I was listening to those conversations. There was tremendous denial. Well, this is temporary. Hitler is just in Austria, and this will all change. Nobody, but nobody believed that our country, that was so good to the Jews, actually-- the only thing as far as I know was that you couldn't be an academic in Yugoslavia. You couldn't be a professor unless you converted to Christian religion. As a Jew, you wouldn't be. But in business, and in any other aspect of life, Jews were accepted everywhere. My parents had some non-Jewish friends. And this helped later. My father, he was taken to concentration camp, actually, only to a camp like a preparation for concentration camp. And these friends of my parents saved him and took him out-- pulled him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war broke out in 1939--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what else do you remember about the conversations that went on in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that there were rumors of concentration camps, that people had put on transports. Because, see, the following happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My parents and I and my sisters, we belonged to the Jewish community. And one day, we were told that some children were coming from Austria to Zagreb. And every family is supposed to take one child to eat with us or live with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1939, 1939 and 40. And we had a young boy my age staying with us. Except at night, because we were three girls, and we didn't have room for a boy. So every night, he went to sleep to another family which was very close by to us. And he was telling us, like, rumors that things are-- people are taken on transports, that he doesn't know where his parents are. This was just a lucky group of children that came. And in 1941, they were sent to Palestine by the Jewish agency. I cannot tell you exactly how it happened. I just can tell you that when we arrived to Israel in 1950, he was standing-- he was already a grown man with a big sign looking for us. Every boat that arrived, he was waiting to see us. And we, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he told his stories in 1939, did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did because we have other family that arrived from Vienna living within the Zagreb. People with money, and they were settled, they came to Zagreb with money. But they were telling us that the situation was awful. And, and anti-Semitism was growing. And they had to escape. They lost positions. So we were one, probably, of the families in the city that knew more than the others, having this connection with Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: When war broke out, other than the-- this boy coming to live with you, did anything else change for you at the end of 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you notice the first signs of danger for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1941, the Germans marched into Zagreb. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were watching from the window. We lived in a very nice part of the city, and they were marching, marching in. The Croatian accepted them with love and adoration. And in no time, a puppet fascist government was formed, which was in preparation already in-- somewhere in Italy. And they were trained by Mussolini. So this puppet government cooperated with the Germans beautifully. So they, they really didn't have to do any dirty job in Zagreb, in Croatia, because it was all done for them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Changes were very fast, very drastic. The Jewish school was blockaded, like, barricaded. We didn't go to school anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: As of when?

speaker: interviewee
text: April, 1941. That's when they marched in. And maybe week-- a few weeks after, the Jewish school was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other changes happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, it started that every Jew had to bring his radio to the government to, I don't know, to the municipality. So we were-- my father was standing in line hours to bring his own radio. This was like the beginning. And then, in no time, the newspapers-- the front page of the newspapers were full with new regulations and racist laws for the Jews. First of all, Jews cannot own any businesses. They had to give it up. And just like that, somebody would walk into their business and take over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews are not allowed to interact with non-Jews. Domestic help, right away, had to be let go. We were not supposed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to talk to anybody, schools-- not to go to schools, not to enter restaurants, not to enter coffee houses. And very shortly after, we were handed the yellow patches, the yellow Star of David. We had to wear it on the back and on the front of our chest, even babies in, in carriages. And this was, like, worn as an emblem of identification and shame. So the little-- as little as possible, we tried to leave the house because we never knew what can happen walking in the street with this yellow star. I remember that, in those days, I was close to 11. I became so independent because basically my parents sent me to do some shopping. With the maid at the beginning, and then later, she had to let go. Because they were afraid to be seen too much. Now the problem in my family was that my-- one of my sisters, the older, she was already at the university. She was 21. She was studying economics. Economics was an extremely communist, socialist university. And she was one of these people that had-- went to demonstrations. And once she was caught with a very big group of young people and put to jail overnight. And she was registered at the police, which was very scary for my parents. They knew that, first of all, who will be deported will be those that have a name registered as socialist in the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back for second before you continue with your sister. When these new regulations and new racist laws came out in the newspaper--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your father lost his business, what, what was going on in your house at the time? What was the conversation like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The conversation was mostly, who did we hear was thrown out of his apartment? Who did we hear was deported? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which young people disappeared?

speaker: interviewer
text: First, we would-- we would hear that some young people would just disappear. And it was, like, almost a, a lottery. Who is going to be? I mean, there was no strategic way you could follow to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no way that you could think, maybe if I do this or that, I won't be one of them. There was no way. It was just very random. It was very-- it was a maddening-- it was a madhouse. You have to understand, even for the Croatian government, the new puppet government, this was new. They were doing things madly, crazily, the way it came to them. It was not yet regulated. It did after. Now, let me tell you what happened to our family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the first-- on the first floor where we lived, the headquarters of Ustasha, which-- Ustasha were the military police of the government. They opened their headquarters. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you can imagine living in a house like this where they were constantly singing, we don't want Jews. Out with them, out with them. There was a certain song that they were singing. And these were young punks that were never good for anything, and now they've got these beautiful positions.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that our days are numbered in that apartment. And what was surprising, people that seemed our friends, that seemed at least our acquaintances, that were not Jewish-- when we would go down the stairs or in the elevator, they never talked to us. They looked away. They looked down. They looked up. It was like some-- we were-- we had a plague, or something. We never heard a good word. You know, this is-- will be over. And and don't worry. And we're on your side-- never. It was-- our experience was not benevolent.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we thought that we are liked.

speaker: interviewer
text: As an 11-year-old, with all of this going on around you, were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. I just felt there is a excitement, that our life is not anymore as it used to be, that-- my parents don't care what I do, where I go. It was a chaos.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do every day when you weren't going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would meet with some friends who were not going to school either [SIGHS] in my house or in their house. And slowly, they were, like, disappearing. We didn't know where-- I had my best friend. She left with her family to Banja Luka, which is in Bosnia, with her parents. People were just leaving, coming. We didn't know. Nobody knew. Nobody was smart to say, you know what, if we follow this, that will help. We didn't know. Everybody was doing his own thing. But let me tell you what really changed fast our situation. Since this apartment was in the same building with the headquarters of this military was, there were people in this office that wanted our apartment. It was convenient to live in the same house and have the children come down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, very soon after the Germans marched into Zagreb, it was Pesach. Somebody knocked at our door. And they walked in in uniforms with bayonets. And they said, very politely, actually, you have to pack your personal belongings in a few hours because this apartment is confiscated. So now-- this was right at the beginning. We had no experience from other friends-- what do you do? We only knew that we had very short hours and that you have to do-- take things that you really cannot be without. So we were running around in this apartment. Everybody was packing. And they were standing there, like, vigilantly and watching what we are taking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we couldn't take anything of big value. We had wonderful encyclopedia and silver. And, and the only thing my father had saved was money before and gold. In those days, as you know, the European Jews, it, it was a security blanket to have a lot of jewelry and gold. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Fine. In a few hours, we were in the street with suitcases.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you packed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My parents-- my mother packed my clothing. She knew what I needed. I was packing my dolls. I had a teddy bear and books. These books kept me going for years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We came down with the suitcases with the elevator-- a few times we went. [SIGHS] And we stood in the street. My mother was a very optimistic person. She was strong, and she was always in crisis, excellent. And my father said, now we go to my brother-- who has a non-Jewish wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far. We could not walk. We took a cab. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we arrived to them, the five of us with suitcases. And we said, we'll stay here until we find a place to, to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: A place to live meant a room. And to find a room was an impossible task. Who wanted to, to rent a room to a Jew? Nobody. This, this was like a miracle to find something. And we did, but it took a long time. So now we lived with this uncle, my aunt, and my cousin. My father became terribly nervous about my sisters. All we heard was that young people were just disappearing at random, especially students, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like end of high school and beginning of university. They were just put on transports, all on some voyage, never heard again. Had your sister been registered with the police by this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Her name was there. And my father wanted to see her out right now. He convinced her to go to Dubrovnik, which was in the Italian zone. But unfortunately, she had a boyfriend who was not Jewish, who was Muslim, at the university. And he convinced her to go with him to Bosnia where he came from, where his family was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she didn't tell my parents where she's going. My father thought he's putting her on a train to Dubrovnik. But she left and went to Bosnia.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was 21. I mean, she was an independent. [SIGHS] She was a very, very independent young woman. She did what she wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date she left?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximate?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was summer. I think it was summer.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't hear from her again, nothing. There was no mail, nothing. Some rumors-- I can't tell you. I don't remember. And I never discussed this with my other sister because I never thought I will have to kind of record it. But I think there was some postcard from her from Sarajevo, which was a big surprise to my parents, and a horrible disappointment, knowing that she is in the middle of the worst part of war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But she was incognito there. And she left with this boyfriend and a girlfriend. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I will backtrack a little bit now. This girlfriend survived the war. And she told us the following. They arrived to Sarajevo. And they were thrown in jail right away. They were beaten up in jail terribly, all three of them. But they didn't say anything. They just didn't talk. They, they didn't have what to say. And the milkman that was bringing them milk into the jail was a partisan. This was already the beginning of underground and the resistance, 1941. And Tito was forming his 1st Proletarian Brigade beginning of 1941. And this brigade consisted of the hard-core communists and, of course, people that were fighting anti-Semitism. There were some Jews there, not few-- many-- and people that were anti-fascist that decided they're going to fight whatever the way they can. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this underground-- somebody from the underground-- because her boyfriend was already connected with the underground-- who was bringing milk into the jail on a cart saved those three people, got them out. And in this cart covered with rags, they get-- got out of the jail. And then she joined the 1st Proletarian Brigade with this girlfriend and with him. And in 1942, unfortunately, she was caught. She had some mission, which this girlfriend didn't know anymore exactly because they were not so much together anymore. She had a mission to transfer some documents because she was one of the few that could type. She was intelligent. So she worked with documents. And they, they sent her to transfer something from one part of Bosnia to another. She was caught by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She was hung in Sarajevo. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] OK. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know much about her until late, late after the war, how she ended. And this is not authentic either. So, that's what we heard. The rumors and-- after the war. But-- because in this brigade very, very few people survived. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back to where you were staying with your aunt and uncle. How long were you and your sister and your parents living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my second sister, my father wanted badly she should leave, too. So they sent her to Belgrade. My mother had a sister who was married to a Serb, non-Jewish. And he was killed right away by the Croatians, but right away. And she went to his family who lived in Belgrade. This aunt and my sister joined her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here we were now living in this house, the three of us. My sisters were gone. My parents were very relieved. And there was something of a small denial already. My father said, well, who will take me and your mother? And you are a child. Maybe we can see what is going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we found a room. This is very vague in my memory, this room where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you tell me about the room, how long did you stay there at your aunt and uncle's?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you found a room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we found a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zagreb. I have no real recollection who these people were. My father was paying them. And we lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the room looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Small.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how long you stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't stay long. One day, my father came home. And he said, I found a connection. We are leaving. There is no hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you there. We'll change the tape and get back to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
